guides.title: CPR Basics
guides.summary: Immediate CPR actions for a collapsed person who is not breathing normally.
steps: Check the scene is safe and ask for help immediately.
Open the airway and check for normal breathing for no more than 10 seconds.
Start chest compressions at a steady pace in the centre of the chest.
Continue CPR until the person recovers, help takes over, or you cannot continue.
guides.notes: Call 000 and use an AED if one is available.

guides.title: Major Bleeding Control
guides.summary: How to control severe bleeding while waiting for emergency services.
steps: Apply firm direct pressure to the wound using a clean dressing or cloth.
Lay the person down if possible and keep them still and warm.
Bandage the dressing firmly without cutting off circulation.
Call 000 and monitor for shock until help arrives.
guides.notes: Use firm direct pressure and do not remove soaked dressings; add more on top.

guides.title: Burns First Aid
guides.summary: Simple first aid for heat, flame, and scald burns.
steps: Cool the burn under cool running water for 20 minutes as soon as possible.
Remove jewellery and restrictive clothing unless it is stuck to the burn.
Cover the burn loosely with a clean non-stick dressing or cling film.
Seek medical care for larger, deeper, facial, airway, or chemical burns.
guides.notes: Do not apply butter, creams, or ice directly to a burn.

guides.title: Fracture Immobilisation
guides.summary: Basic steps for suspected fractures after falls, crashes, or structural damage.
steps: Support the injured area and stop the person from using it.
Immobilise the limb in the position found using padding or a sling if appropriate.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling if available.
Seek urgent medical care, especially for severe pain, deformity, or reduced circulation.
guides.notes: Only move the person if they are in immediate danger.

guides.title: Snake Bite First Aid
guides.summary: Pressure immobilisation steps for a suspected venomous snake bite.
steps: Keep the person still and calm to slow venom spread.
Apply a firm pressure bandage over the bite site and the whole limb if possible.
Immobilise the limb with a splint and keep the person lying down.
Call 000 urgently and do not let the person walk.
guides.notes: Do not wash the bite site because venom traces may assist identification.

sections.title: Immediate Actions
sections.summary: The first two minutes matter more than trying to identify the snake.
steps: Move the person only if there is ongoing danger from the snake or the scene.
Keep them lying down and reassure them so heart rate stays as low as possible.
Send for help and be ready to call 000 early rather than waiting for symptoms.

sections.title: Pressure Immobilisation
sections.summary: Bandage the entire limb firmly, then splint it so movement stays minimal.
steps: Start at the bite site if visible and wrap a firm pressure bandage over the whole limb.
Keep toes or fingers visible if practical so circulation can still be checked.
Apply a splint over the bandage and keep the limb still until medical help arrives.

diagrams.title: Pressure Bandage Wrap
diagrams.caption: Wrap firmly over and above the bite, then continue over the full limb before splinting.

guides.title: Household Evacuation Quick Start
guides.summary: A fast sequence for leaving home safely when warnings escalate.
steps: Take people, pets, medicines, documents, and go-bags first.
Shut doors and windows if time allows, and turn off utilities if advised and safe.
Send one quick status message to your agreed contact when departing.
Use the planned route and do not stop to collect low-priority items.
guides.notes: Leaving early is usually safer than leaving late into smoke, floodwater, or heavy wind.

guides.title: Shelter-in-Place Steps
guides.summary: How to set up safely when staying put is the safest option.
steps: Move everyone to the strongest practical part of the building.
Bring water, food, medicines, lighting, radio, and phone charging options together.
Limit door openings and keep pets secured near the family group.
Review exits and what would trigger a move if conditions worsen.
guides.notes: Keep listening to official warnings because a shelter-in-place decision can change.

guides.title: Utility Shutoff Safety
guides.summary: How to think about shutting off power, gas, and water safely after damage or flooding.
steps: Know where the main switches and valves are before an emergency occurs.
Turn off power if floodwater or serious wiring damage is present and it is safe to reach the switch.
Turn off water if broken pipes, contamination, or structural damage are suspected.
Do not restore utilities until a qualified person or official advice says it is safe.
guides.notes: If you smell gas, leave immediately and contact the utility from a safe distance.

guides.title: Safe Cleanup After Disaster
guides.summary: Safe priorities for household cleanup after flood, storm, or earthquake damage.
steps: Photograph damage before major cleanup if insurance evidence is needed.
Check for structural, electrical, chemical, and animal hazards before entering.
Work in short sessions, ventilate the area, and wash hands frequently.
Dispose of contaminated food, water-damaged medicines, and unsafe electrical items.
guides.notes: Wear gloves, sturdy shoes, and respiratory protection where dust, mould, or contaminated water are present.

guides.title: Official Warning Monitoring
guides.summary: How to keep warnings flowing when power and connectivity become unreliable.
steps: Identify the main warning sources you trust before an event starts.
Keep a radio ready as a fallback if mobile data becomes unreliable.
Check the warning level, location, and timeframe, not just the headline.
Act on evacuation or shelter instructions early instead of waiting for visual confirmation.
guides.notes: Cross-check information with official agencies when possible and avoid rumours.

guides.title: Safe Tarp Shelter Setup
guides.summary: Basic tarp shelter setup for temporary cover in wind and rain.
steps: Choose ground that is above obvious runoff and away from overhead hazards.
Pitch the ridgeline tight and angle the tarp so water runs away from sleeping areas.
Secure corners low and firm to reduce wind flap and tears.
Keep cooking and open flames well away from the shelter fabric.
guides.notes: Do not camp under dead branches, unstable slopes, or flood-prone creek beds.

sections.title: Pick the Site
sections.summary: A perfect tarp shape cannot compensate for a poor campsite.
steps: Avoid creek beds, drainage lines, widow-makers, and loose rockfall slopes.
Face the low side of the shelter away from the prevailing wind and runoff.
Keep enough room for gear, cooking, and a dry entry point.

sections.title: Build the Ridgeline
sections.summary: A tight ridgeline is what makes a tarp feel solid instead of floppy.
steps: Run a line between stable anchors and tension it before clipping or tying the tarp.
Pull the tarp tight over the ridge so water sheds instead of pooling.
Peg out the corners low and re-tension once the tarp takes shape.

diagrams.title: Ridgeline Shelter
diagrams.caption: Set a taut ridgeline first, then pitch the tarp into the wind with the low side protected.

guides.title: Campfire Safety Without Mains
guides.summary: Safer fire use when cooking or heating away from normal facilities.
steps: Clear a safe area around the fire or use an approved fire pit.
Keep water, sand, or a shovel within reach before lighting anything.
Never leave flames unattended and keep children and pets supervised.
Extinguish fully until cold before leaving or sleeping.
guides.notes: Observe local fire bans and use a stove instead when conditions are hot, dry, or windy.

guides.title: Field Food Hygiene
guides.summary: Reducing foodborne illness when kitchens, sinks, or refrigeration are limited.
steps: Keep raw and ready-to-eat food separate.
Use the coldest practical storage for perishable items and discard anything unsafe.
Cook food thoroughly and reheat leftovers only if they have remained safe to store.
Wash hands or use sanitiser before handling meals.
guides.notes: Clean hands and utensils matter more as temperatures rise and water quality becomes uncertain.

guides.title: Improvised Signal Methods
guides.summary: Ways to improve your chances of being seen or heard when communications fail.
steps: Use whistle blasts, lights, mirrors, or bright fabric to attract attention.
Place signals where they can be seen from roads, tracks, or the air.
Keep signals simple and repeated rather than constantly changing them.
Stay near a safe, known position unless moving clearly improves your safety.
guides.notes: Only leave markers or fires where they do not create new danger.

guides.title: Basic Knot Selection
guides.summary: A practical set of knots for shelter, gear retention, and emergency rigging.
steps: Use a reef knot for joining similar ropes where a load is stable.
Use a clove hitch for quick attachment to poles or rails.
Use a bowline for a fixed loop that is easy to untie later.
Check every knot under light load before relying on it.
guides.notes: Practice knots before you need them so you can tie them quickly in poor light or bad weather.

sections.title: Choose the Right Knot
sections.summary: Use the simplest knot that matches the job instead of forcing one knot to do everything.
steps: Pick a bowline when you need a fixed loop that should still untie easily.
Pick a clove hitch when you are fastening onto a rail, pole, or stake quickly.
Pick a reef knot only for joining two similar ends under steady load.

sections.title: Check Before Trusting
sections.summary: A badly dressed knot can slip even if it is the right type.
steps: Dress the knot neatly so each turn lies flat instead of crossing awkwardly.
Pull on the loaded sections first, then test the tail ends.
Leave enough tail length so movement or vibration does not work the knot loose.

diagrams.title: Reef Knot
diagrams.caption: Use only for joining similar ends with a stable load, not for critical life support.

diagrams.title: Clove Hitch
diagrams.caption: Quick to place on poles, rails, or ridgelines when you need fast attachment.

diagrams.title: Bowline
diagrams.caption: Creates a fixed loop that is secure under load but still easier to untie later.

guides.title: Navigate with Map and Compass
guides.summary: Simple map and compass use when GPS is unavailable or unreliable.
steps: Orient the map to north using a compass.
Identify major terrain or road features that match the map.
Set a simple bearing or route segment to the next clear checkpoint.
Stop and recheck often rather than pushing on while uncertain.
guides.notes: Navigation is easiest when done early, in good light, before fatigue builds.

sections.title: Orient First
sections.summary: Get the map and the ground agreeing before you decide where to walk.
steps: Turn the map until north on the page lines up with the compass needle.
Find one obvious terrain feature or road that you can confirm visually.
Use that feature to confirm you are not mirrored or rotated.

sections.title: Move in Short Legs
steps: Pick a nearby checkpoint you can clearly identify before moving off.
Walk to it, stop, and compare the map to the ground again.
Repeat instead of trying to hold one long uncertain line through difficult country.

diagrams.title: Simple Bearing Check
diagrams.caption: Orient the map, set a short bearing, then move checkpoint by checkpoint instead of guessing.

sources.title: Original offline map and compass quick guide

guides.title: Set Simple Rally Points
guides.summary: How to choose easy rally points for families or travel groups.
steps: Pick landmarks that are easy to identify in low light or poor weather.
Name a primary and backup point before splitting up.
Tell every member how long to wait at the point before moving.
Avoid rally points in floodways, near fuel, or under unstable structures.
guides.notes: Choose locations that are obvious, safe, and accessible from more than one direction.

guides.title: Route Planning Without GPS
guides.summary: Planning alternate routes when apps, charging, or coverage are unavailable.
steps: Review the main route and at least one alternate before departure.
Avoid low crossings, single-access roads, and heavily wooded bottlenecks when hazards increase.
Mark fuel, shelter, and hospital options along the route.
Tell a trusted contact which route you expect to use.
guides.notes: Paper maps and local knowledge are often faster than troubleshooting a failing app.

guides.title: Night Navigation Safety
guides.summary: Reducing navigation mistakes and injury risk after dark.
steps: Use headlamps or torches that preserve enough peripheral vision to spot hazards.
Stay on obvious roads, tracks, or marked paths whenever possible.
Stop regularly to confirm bearings and landmarks instead of guessing.
Avoid creek crossings, unstable edges, and unfamiliar shortcuts in darkness.
guides.notes: Moving slowly with deliberate checks is safer than trying to maintain daytime pace.

guides.title: Vehicle Trip Check-In Routine
guides.summary: A simple communication routine for remote or hazard-season road travel.
steps: Send a departure message with route, destination, and ETA.
Update the contact if you change route or stop unexpectedly.
Keep the contact informed if signal is intermittent or battery is low.
Send a final arrival message so the contact does not continue monitoring.
guides.notes: If the check-in is missed, the contact should know when to escalate concern.

guides.title: Boil, Filter, and Disinfect Water
guides.summary: A practical order for making uncertain water safer to drink.
steps: Filter or settle dirty water to remove visible debris.
Boil water when possible or use the treatment method recommended for your gear.
Store treated water in a clean container with a lid.
Keep untreated water separate from treated water to avoid cross-contamination.
guides.notes: Clearer water is easier to disinfect, so settle or pre-filter muddy water first.

sections.title: Clear the Water First
sections.summary: Treatment works better when the water is less muddy.
steps: Let heavy sediment settle, then pour off the clearer water.
Strain debris through clean cloth or a pre-filter before using your main treatment method.
Keep the dirty-water container separate from your treated-water container.

sections.title: Treat and Store
sections.summary: Choose the treatment method that matches the gear and fuel you still have.
steps: Boil whenever practical because it is the clearest field method when you have heat available.
If you are using a filter or chemical treatment, follow that gear's directions exactly.
Seal treated water and label it mentally or physically so it is not mixed with raw water later.

diagrams.title: Settle, Filter, Then Treat
diagrams.caption: Clear debris first, then apply your main treatment method before storing in a clean container.

guides.title: Store Water Safely in Heat
guides.summary: How to reduce spoilage and container damage during hot weather.
steps: Use food-grade containers that seal properly.
Store water out of direct sun and away from fuel or chemicals.
Rotate water on a schedule and inspect containers for damage.
Carry only what your container can handle safely if left in a hot car.
guides.notes: Heat shortens the life of many containers, especially in vehicles and sheds.

guides.title: Ration Water Without Dehydration
guides.summary: Balancing water conservation with health and safe decision-making.
steps: Set a daily water plan for each person and review it morning and evening.
Reduce unnecessary activity during the hottest part of the day.
Use shaded rest periods and lightweight clothing to slow water loss.
Watch for headache, dizziness, dark urine, and confusion as warning signs.
guides.notes: Children, older adults, and people working in heat often need closer monitoring.

guides.title: Collect Rainwater Safely
guides.summary: Basic rainwater collection practices for emergency household use.
steps: Use clean containers and collect from the cleanest available surface.
Discard the first dirty runoff if possible.
Keep lids on storage containers to reduce contamination.
Treat collected water before drinking unless you are certain it is safe.
guides.notes: Collection does not guarantee potability, so treat water when safety is uncertain.

guides.title: After-Flood Drinking Water Checks
guides.summary: How to decide when to rely on stored water after flooding.
steps: Use stored drinking water first while supply safety is uncertain.
Do not drink water that smells unusual, looks dirty, or comes from damaged plumbing.
Treat suspect water using your backup method if no safer option exists.
Flush and inspect plumbing only when authorities say conditions are safe.
guides.notes: Use official advice where available because contamination is often not visible.

guides.title: Bushfire Leave-Early Plan
guides.summary: A leave-early plan for bushfire days and escalating warnings.
steps: Check weather, fire danger, and local warnings well before the day peaks.
Load go-bags, water, medicines, pets, and documents before conditions worsen.
Leave when your trigger point is reached rather than waiting for visible flame or smoke.
Use the planned route and tell your contact once you are safe.
guides.notes: Road conditions can change quickly, so the safest departure is usually the earliest one.

guides.title: Fire Extinguisher Basics
guides.summary: When and how to use an extinguisher on a small, manageable fire.
steps: Position yourself with a clear exit behind you.
Aim at the base of the fire rather than the flames.
Use short bursts and sweep side to side.
Stop and leave if the fire does not reduce quickly.
guides.notes: If the fire is growing, smoky, or blocking your escape, leave immediately instead.

guides.title: Fire Blanket Use
guides.summary: Using a fire blanket on a pan fire or clothing fire.
steps: Pull the blanket tabs and shield your hands behind the top edge.
Place the blanket over the flames from the near edge away from you.
Leave the blanket in place until everything is fully cool.
Call emergency services if the fire spreads or anyone is burned.
guides.notes: Turn off the heat if it is safe to do so and never carry a burning pan.

guides.title: Ember Attack Home Actions
guides.summary: Actions for ember attack around a house when it is still safe to respond.
steps: Move quickly to extinguish spot fires in gutters, decks, and garden edges.
Keep doors and windows closed to reduce ember entry.
Wear sturdy clothing, gloves, and respiratory protection if available.
Continue monitoring the structure for new ignitions after the front passes.
guides.notes: Only take these actions if local advice and your plan say it is safe to remain.

guides.title: Smoke Exposure Reduction
guides.summary: Reducing smoke exposure during bushfire and heavy-cleanup conditions.
steps: Stay indoors with doors and windows closed when smoke is heavy outside.
Use a P2 mask if you need to move outdoors and can wear one safely.
Limit strenuous outdoor activity during dense smoke periods.
Seek medical advice promptly if breathing symptoms worsen.
guides.notes: People with asthma, heart disease, and other medical conditions may need earlier action.

guides.title: Heat Exhaustion vs Heat Stroke
guides.summary: How to recognise escalating heat illness and respond early.
steps: Move the person to shade or a cooler place immediately.
Cool the body with wet cloths, airflow, or cool water if available.
Give small sips of water only if the person is alert and not vomiting.
Call 000 urgently if the person is confused, collapses, or becomes hot and dry.
guides.notes: Heat stroke is a medical emergency; call 000 if it is suspected.

guides.title: Assemble Family Medications
guides.summary: Keeping household medicines organised for rapid movement or sheltering.
steps: List each person’s medications, doses, and prescribing doctor.
Store medicines in one clearly marked, easy-to-grab pouch or container.
Include administration items such as spacers, measuring syringes, or sharps disposal if needed.
Pack the medication pouch into the go-bag first during evacuation.
guides.notes: Check expiry dates and repeat prescriptions before high-risk seasons.

guides.title: Hygiene When Water Is Limited
guides.summary: Simple hygiene priorities when water is scarce or services are disrupted.
steps: Use clean water and soap for critical handwashing when possible.
Prioritise hand cleaning before food prep, wound care, and eating.
Use wipes or sanitiser as a backup but clean visible dirt off first where possible.
Bag waste and dirty items promptly so living areas stay safer.
guides.notes: Protecting hand hygiene around food, wounds, and toileting gives the highest return.

guides.title: Wound Cleaning and Dressing
guides.summary: Basic wound care for cuts and scrapes in dirty conditions.
steps: Wash hands and control bleeding before cleaning the wound.
Rinse the wound gently with clean water and remove obvious loose dirt.
Apply an appropriate dressing and secure it without excessive pressure.
Change the dressing if it becomes wet or dirty and watch for infection signs.
guides.notes: Seek medical help early if the wound is deep, heavily contaminated, or not controllable.

guides.title: Asthma Attack Response
guides.summary: Immediate action for a suspected asthma attack.
steps: Sit the person upright and stay calm.
Give reliever puffs through a spacer if available, following the person’s plan.
Repeat doses as needed while monitoring breathing and speech.
Seek urgent medical help if the person worsens, cannot speak normally, or blue lips appear.
guides.notes: If symptoms are severe or not improving, call 000 without delay.

guides.title: Cool a Home Without Power
guides.summary: Practical ways to limit indoor heat load during outages.
steps: Close blinds, curtains, and external doors before the hottest part of the day.
Use shaded airflow at night and early morning when outdoor air is cooler.
Move people, pets, and heat-sensitive medicines to the coolest practical room.
Use cool water, damp cloths, and light clothing to support heat loss.
guides.notes: Focus on the coolest room and reduce unnecessary movement around the house.

guides.title: Prevent Dehydration in Extreme Heat
guides.summary: Hydration habits that reduce risk during hot weather and smoke events.
steps: Drink regularly through the day rather than waiting until very thirsty.
Use shade and scheduled rests to slow fluid loss.
Eat light meals and include salty food if sweating heavily and medically appropriate.
Check urine colour and how people are feeling during the day.
guides.notes: Older adults, children, and people working outdoors may need closer prompting to drink.

guides.title: Protect Medicines from Heat
guides.summary: How to keep medicines effective during hot weather and outages.
steps: Check which medicines require strict temperature control.
Use insulated storage or cooling bricks only if they do not freeze the medicine.
Move heat-sensitive items out of cars, tin sheds, and window sills.
Call a pharmacist if the medicine has been exposed to temperatures outside guidance.
guides.notes: Always follow the storage directions on the packaging or pharmacist advice.

guides.title: Safe Work-Rest Cycles
guides.summary: Balancing urgent jobs with safe recovery in hot conditions.
steps: Do heavy tasks early or late in the day if possible.
Break work into short blocks with hydration and cooling breaks.
Watch partners for slowing, confusion, or irritability.
Stop immediately if dizziness, cramps, or headache develop.
guides.notes: Short, regular breaks are more effective than pushing until exhaustion.

guides.title: Recognise Heat Risk in Children and Older Adults
guides.summary: Spotting higher heat risk in people who may not self-report early symptoms.
steps: Monitor hydration, alertness, and the temperature of living spaces.
Dress people in light clothing and keep them in the coolest practical room.
Offer regular fluids and cooling opportunities throughout the day.
Escalate early if confusion, weakness, or poor urine output develops.
guides.notes: Check on higher-risk people proactively rather than waiting for them to ask.

guides.title: Severe Storm Room Setup
guides.summary: Setting up a safer internal room before damaging winds arrive.
steps: Choose an internal room away from windows and unsecured outdoor items.
Move water, lighting, radio, medicines, shoes, and chargers into the room.
Keep pets, helmets if available, and sturdy clothing nearby.
Stay inside until official advice says the worst conditions have passed.
guides.notes: Avoid rooms with large windows or lightweight roof structures if stronger options exist.

guides.title: Cyclone Pre-Landfall Actions
guides.summary: Short actions to take before strong winds and heavy rain arrive.
steps: Secure or bring in outdoor furniture, bins, and loose equipment.
Top up water containers, fuel, and power banks early.
Move vehicles under cover or away from trees and flood-prone areas.
Shelter in your strongest practical room before peak conditions arrive.
guides.notes: Finish outside jobs early; late movement outdoors is where injuries rise quickly.

guides.title: Generator Safety After Storm
guides.summary: Using a generator safely after storms and outages.
steps: Place the generator outdoors in a dry, ventilated position away from openings.
Use heavy-duty leads suited to the load and protect them from water damage.
Refuel only when the generator is off and cool.
Switch appliances on gradually instead of loading everything at once.
guides.notes: Carbon monoxide can kill quickly, so never run a generator indoors or in a garage.

guides.title: Protect Devices and Documents Before Storm
guides.summary: Reducing damage to electronics and paperwork before heavy weather.
steps: Charge devices and then unplug non-essential electronics before unstable weather peaks.
Move important papers into a waterproof pouch or sealed container.
Store small electronics above floor level and away from likely leaks.
Photograph the home condition and key items before the event if time allows.
guides.notes: Early packing is easier than trying to protect items during wind or water entry.

guides.title: Downed Powerline Safety
guides.summary: Basic safety around fallen powerlines and damaged electrical infrastructure.
steps: Stay well clear of fallen lines, wet ground, and anything touching the line.
Warn others away without approaching the hazard yourself.
Report the line to emergency services or the electricity utility promptly.
Do not drive over or under a fallen line or cordon.
guides.notes: Always assume fallen lines are live and dangerous.

guides.title: Flood Evacuation Timing
guides.summary: Why early movement matters when floodwater is rising or roads are cutting off.
steps: Watch for local warnings, creek rises, and access road closures early.
Leave while roads are still open and visibility is good.
Move to higher ground or your planned safe location without stopping for low-priority tasks.
Tell your contact when you have arrived safely.
guides.notes: If you wait until water is visible at the door, route options may already be gone.

guides.title: Turn Around, Don’t Drown
guides.summary: A reminder of why floodwater crossing by foot or car is so dangerous.
steps: Do not drive into water covering the road, even if another vehicle has crossed.
Do not walk into moving floodwater because footing can disappear quickly.
Find another route or wait on safe high ground if the road is cut.
Call for help if you become trapped rather than taking a risky crossing.
guides.notes: Depth, flow, road damage, and contamination are often impossible to judge accurately.

guides.title: Entering a Water-Damaged Home
guides.summary: Safety checks before re-entering a flooded building.
steps: Check the outside for structural movement, sagging, or obvious collapse risk.
Do not enter until electricity and gas hazards are assessed or isolated.
Wear protective footwear, gloves, and respiratory protection if mould or sewage is present.
Ventilate the building and move out unsafe food, medicines, and soft furnishings promptly.
guides.notes: Flood-damaged homes may contain hidden structural, electrical, and contamination hazards.

guides.title: Mould and Contamination Cleanup
guides.summary: Reducing health risks during post-flood cleanup.
steps: Remove wet porous items that cannot be cleaned and dried quickly.
Clean hard surfaces with detergent and follow local health advice on disinfectants.
Dry the space as quickly as possible with airflow and dehumidification if available.
Take breaks and avoid stirring dust or mould without protection.
guides.notes: Large mould contamination or sewage exposure may require professional advice.

guides.title: Sandbag Placement Basics
guides.summary: A simple method for getting better value from limited sandbags.
steps: Clear debris from the doorway or opening first.
Lay the first row tightly with bag openings tucked under.
Stagger higher rows like brickwork and press bags together firmly.
Use plastic sheeting behind the bags when available for a better seal.
guides.notes: Sandbags reduce shallow ingress but do not make a building waterproof.

guides.title: Pantry Damper on a Hotplate
guides.summary: A simple damper method for outages, camps, and basic pantry cooking.
steps: Mix self-raising flour, a pinch of salt, and enough water to form a soft dough.
Shape the dough into a thick round and dust lightly so it does not stick.
Cook it in a covered pan, camp oven, or on a hotplate with gentle heat until the centre is cooked through.
Let it rest briefly before slicing so the crumb finishes setting.
guides.notes: Keep the heat moderate. Fast, high heat can burn the outside before the middle cooks.

sections.title: Mix a Simple Dough
sections.summary: Damper works because the dough stays simple and forgiving.
steps: Start with flour, a pinch of salt, and optional milk powder or a little fat if you have it.
Add water gradually and stop as soon as the dough comes together without becoming sticky slurry.
Do not knead hard; just bring it together and shape it.

sections.title: Cook Gently
sections.summary: Lower heat and a lid are what make stovetop damper workable.
steps: Preheat the pan or plate gently rather than blasting it with high flame.
Cover the dough so heat circulates and the centre cooks more evenly.
Turn once if needed and listen for scorching before it becomes blackened.

diagrams.title: Damper Pantry Ratio
diagrams.caption: Keep the dough simple: flour first, water added slowly, and gentle heat with a lid.

guides.title: Skillet Bread Without an Oven
guides.summary: An easy stovetop loaf method when you have a pan, lid, and basic ingredients.
steps: Mix flour, yeast, salt, and water into a sticky dough.
Let the dough rise until visibly puffed if you have time and warmth.
Cook in a covered pan over low heat until the base is firm, then turn if needed.
Rest the loaf before cutting so steam finishes the centre.
guides.notes: If fuel is tight, make smaller flat loaves rather than one thick loaf.

sections.title: Mix and Rise
sections.summary: A wetter dough usually cooks better in a lidded pan than a dry heavy dough.
steps: Mix until no dry flour remains, then cover and let the dough rise if time allows.
Use the warmest safe place you have so the dough becomes lighter before cooking.
If you cannot wait for a full rise, flatten the dough thinner to help it cook through.

sections.title: Cook with a Lid
sections.summary: The lid traps heat and does some of the job the oven would normally handle.
steps: Keep the flame or burner low once the pan is hot.
Rotate or turn the loaf if your stove has hot spots.
Check the centre before serving and return it to the pan if it is still gummy.

diagrams.title: Skillet Bread Setup
diagrams.caption: A covered pan and lower heat help the loaf cook through instead of scorching underneath.

guides.title: Blackout Scones from a Basic Pantry
guides.summary: Quick scones when you need fast food from flour, leavening, and a little fat.
steps: Rub a small amount of butter or oil through flour, then add liquid just until a dough forms.
Pat the dough thick and cut it into rounds or wedges.
Bake or cook on a hotplate with moderate heat until risen and lightly browned.
Serve warm or let them cool fully before packing for later.
guides.notes: Handle the dough lightly. Heavy kneading makes scones dense.

diagrams.title: Cut-and-Cook Scones
diagrams.caption: Bring the dough together gently, cut it, then cook with moderate heat rather than hard browning.

guides.title: Grow Salad Greens Fast
guides.summary: A quick-start method for leafy greens when you want edible harvests fast.
steps: Use a shallow bed, tray, or container with loose potting mix and reliable drainage.
Sow small amounts often instead of planting one large batch all at once.
Keep the soil evenly damp while seeds sprout and while leaves stay tender.
Harvest outer leaves first so the plants keep producing.
guides.notes: Greens bolt quickly in heat, so give them morning sun and afternoon protection when summer arrives.

sections.title: Plant for Repeat Harvest
sections.summary: A few small beds or trays usually feed better than one oversized planting.
steps: Leave enough spacing for airflow even when the plants are still tiny.
Stagger sowing dates so every container does not mature at once.
Pick outer leaves regularly instead of waiting for one giant harvest.

diagrams.title: Simple Raised-Bed Layout
diagrams.caption: A spaced-out bed or planter makes watering and repeat harvest easier than overcrowding seedlings.

guides.title: Start Seeds in Small Spaces
guides.summary: Use trays, recycled containers, and a sunny window to build seedlings before transplanting.
steps: Use clean trays or small containers with drainage holes and a light seed-raising mix.
Sow shallowly, label the tray, and keep the surface damp but not waterlogged.
Move seedlings into stronger light as soon as they germinate so they do not stretch weakly.
Harden them off gradually before planting outdoors.
guides.notes: Overwatering and weak light kill more seedlings than minor cold snaps.

diagrams.title: Seed Tray Layout
diagrams.caption: Keep cells evenly moist and give young seedlings light early so they stay compact and strong.

guides.title: Grow Potatoes in Bags
guides.summary: A compact method for producing potatoes when you have little ground space.
steps: Start with a deep bag or container that drains well and add a base layer of growing mix.
Set seed potatoes or cut pieces with healthy eyes on the mix and cover lightly.
Add more mix as shoots grow so new tubers form higher up the stem.
Keep the bag evenly watered and harvest once the tops die back.
guides.notes: Do not let the bag dry hard during tuber formation or the crop will stay small.

diagrams.title: Layered Potato Bag
diagrams.caption: Start low, then hill up the growing mix as the stems rise so more potatoes can form.
